commands.review_summary:
This PR restores non-terminal commitment extraction batches to the front of the in-memory queue, shares drain scheduling, and adds regression tests for restore, ordering, terminal failure, and full-queue retry cases.

PR surface: Source +18, Tests +243. Total +261 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence reproduction path: enqueue eligible commitment items, make the extractor throw a non-terminal error after the batch is spliced, and current main loses the batch. I did not execute tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] No automated repair target: the remaining blocker is contributor or maintainer proof review, not a concrete code defect.

target.title: fix(commitments): preserve extraction batch on transient failure
target.body:
## Summary

The commitment extraction queue drains in batches: `drainCommitmentExtractionQueue` does `queue.splice(0, batchMaxItems)` to take a batch off the queue, then runs the extractor. If the extractor threw a **non-terminal** error (e.g. a transient model/network error), the `catch` only re-threw ŌĆö the already-dequeued batch was never put back, so those extraction items were silently lost and never retried.

This fixes the data-loss window with a minimal change:

- **Non-terminal extraction failure no longer drops the already-dequeued batch.** The batch is restored to the front of the queue in original order (`queue.unshift(...batch)`) and the error is rethrown so the existing caller still logs it; the next drain reprocesses the same batch in order.
- **Terminal extraction errors keep their existing drop/stop behavior.** Model/auth failures (`No API key found`, `OAuth token refresh failed`, etc.) still go through `openTerminalFailureCooldown` (cooldown + drop the agent's queued items) and rethrow ŌĆö unchanged.
- **Retry scheduling stays healthy even when the queue was full after a restore.** The single-slot debounce is extracted into one helper and is now also invoked from the queue-overflow branch. Without this, a queue left at `queueMaxItems` by a restored batch could never schedule another drain (every later enqueue returns early at the overflow guard before the old scheduling line), leaving the restored batch stuck forever.

Net production change is `src/commitments/runtime.ts` only (+26/ŌłÆ8); the rest is tests.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** A non-terminal failure in `drainCommitmentExtractionQueue` permanently lost the dequeued extraction batch (no restore, no retry). After the fix the batch is restored and retried; terminal failures still drop/stop; the debounce still schedules a retry when the queue is full.
- **Real environment tested:** Deterministic unit / failure-injection tests against the real `drainCommitmentExtractionQueue` / `enqueueCommitmentExtraction` runtime with an injected `extractBatch` and a recorded `setTimer` (this is pure in-memory queue/scheduler logic; there is no channel/provider surface to exercise live).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` (plus adjacent `src/commitments/extraction.test.ts src/commitments/store.test.ts`); `tsgo` core + test-src lanes; `oxfmt --check`; `oxlint`.
- **Evidence after fix:** `runtime.test.ts` ŌåÆ 11 passed (7 pre-existing + 4 new); adjacent commitments tests ŌåÆ 15 passed; `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:test:src` exit 0; `oxfmt --check` clean; `oxlint` exit 0. New tests:
  - *restores and reprocesses a batch after a non-terminal extractor failure* ŌĆö first drain rejects, store stays empty, retry drain resolves and reprocesses the **same** item ids; a third drain is a no-op (no duplicate extraction), store has exactly the two expected dedupe keys (no duplicate persistence).
  - *restores a failed batch to the front, preserving order across batches* ŌĆö 10 items (> `batchMaxItems` = 8); the restored batch lands ahead of the tail, so the observed `sourceMessageId` order across successful runs is `m0..m9`.
  - *keeps the existing drop/stop behavior on terminal extraction failures* ŌĆö terminal throw, then a retry drain resolves to 0 and the extractor is not re-invoked (dropped, not restored).
  - *schedules a retry when a non-terminal failure leaves the queue full* ŌĆö the debounce schedules exactly once while filling; after a fired-then-failed drain, an overflow enqueue still schedules a retry, and the full queue then drains cleanly (in-flight/`draining` state healthy).
- **Observed result after fix:** Non-terminal failures preserve and retry the batch in order with no duplicate persistence/extraction; terminal failures keep dropping/stopping; a full queue still drains after a restore.
- **What was not tested:** No live channel/provider end-to-end run (the change is internal queue logic with no user-visible surface). `hydrateBatch()` throwing before the batch is consumed, bounded-retry/backoff, and a broader retry redesign are intentionally **out of scope** (see below).

## Scope

Single correctness/data-loss fix in `src/commitments/runtime.ts` plus its colocated test. Out-of-scope follow-ups (not addressed here):

- `hydrateBatch()` throwing *before* the `splice`d batch reaches the extractor is a separate loss window.
- Bounded retry / backoff for repeated non-terminal failures.
- Any broader extraction-queue retry redesign.

## No ClawBench

This is a correctness / data-loss fix, not a latency or token-efficiency change, so there is no performance claim to benchmark. Proof is deterministic via unit / invariant / failure-injection tests.

## No CHANGELOG

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit: this is a contributor PR and release notes are generated at release time; the user-facing context lives in this PR body.
target.author: masatohoshino
